jokes.text: Security guard pretends to be a record producer to get Abbi and Ilana to stop drumming, then immediately deflates the setup
jokes.setup: A man approaches two women drumming and launches into an enthusiastic pitch about being a record producer who has been searching for exactly two drummers for his new project — building genuine excitement
jokes.punchline: "No, I'm not serious. I work here, and I need you guys to leave." The elaborate compliment was purely instrumental
jokes.explanation: Tight cold-open misdirection. The guard commits fully to the bit before puncturing it, which is the right choice — if he'd half-committed it would be flat. The payoff is efficient and the deflation of Abbi and Ilana's genuine hope adds a layer of cringe. Not exceptional but a solid opener.

jokes.text: "Cocaine and incest, baby. Can't get enough."
jokes.setup: Ilana enthusiastically declares she loves Scarface and that they just watched it
jokes.punchline: Her reductive summary of Scarface's appeal is 'cocaine and incest' — delivered with the same energy as someone saying they love the cinematography
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable, perfectly Ilana. The joke works on three levels: it's an accurate-ish reduction of Scarface, the cheerful delivery of 'incest' is shocking, and 'Can't get enough' as a tag is perfectly calibrated enthusiasm. Could only come from Ilana. Economy is excellent — five words do the heavy lifting. Borderline 8 on craft, justified by character integration and quotability.

jokes.text: "Sometimes it's the less you know, though... you know?" followed by Abbi's flat "No."
jokes.setup: Abbi suggests Food Inc. is important and eye-opening
jokes.punchline: Ilana delivers a half-formed anti-intellectual dismissal with false profundity; Abbi's single-word rejection lands as the actual punchline
jokes.explanation: The two-beat structure is tight: Ilana's 'you know?' invites agreement, Abbi's 'No.' is the perfect straight-man rejection. The grammar failure in 'the less you know' adds a layer of unintentional self-parody. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Immediate pivot to obsessing over what food will be at the dog wedding
jokes.setup: Abbi has just tried to have a meaningful conversation about food documentaries
jokes.punchline: Ilana skips entirely to 'what food will be at the dog wedding today' — the juxtaposition of rejecting food ethics while immediately fixating on food, and the casual normalization of a 'dog wedding,' lands together
jokes.explanation: Two jokes compressed into one: the irony of pivoting from food ethics to snack fixation, and the completely casual mention of a 'dog wedding' as though it's unremarkable. Neither is individually exceptional but together they work.

jokes.text: Watching the dog wedding e-vite video and declaring it 'a masterpiece'
jokes.setup: They decide to rewatch the e-vite for a dog wedding
jokes.punchline: One of them responds to it with the aesthetic language of serious cinema: 'It really is a masterpiece'
jokes.explanation: Light absurdist beat — applying serious critical language to a dog wedding e-vite. Functional.

jokes.text: "Why is that shot in there? It's animated, it didn't need to be in there at all."
jokes.setup: They're watching a dog wedding e-vite as though it's a film
jokes.punchline: Abbi delivers a genuine, specific film-criticism complaint about an e-vite's production choices — the seriousness of the critique applied to the absurd subject is the joke
jokes.explanation: The joke escalates the 'masterpiece' line by getting specific — a real criticism of an animated shot's narrative necessity in a dog wedding e-vite. The specificity elevates it above the previous beat.

jokes.text: Immediate pivot from film criticism to 'people steal people's dogs and sell them' with a Judge Judy reference
jokes.setup: They are having an engaged conversation about the e-vite's cinematic qualities
jokes.punchline: Ilana introduces a completely unrelated dark factoid about dog theft as a downer, then cites 'Judge Judy' as her source
jokes.explanation: Good non-sequitur with the added joke that Judge Judy is her primary news source for dog crime. The 'not to be like a downer' prefix before an actual downer is a reliable but well-executed structure.

jokes.text: Judge Judy's net worth is bleeped — the number is so large it's treated as profanity
jokes.setup: They've been shocked by 18 seasons; now they look up her net worth
jokes.punchline: The number itself is censored as though it's obscene — implying it's too obscene/vulgar to broadcast
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-joke that uses the show's own censorship convention against itself. Bleeping a dollar figure as though wealth is as obscene as profanity is smart, original, and lands instantly. The visual reaction beat that follows ('Keep that face') extends it well.

jokes.text: "Wait, keep that face. I gotta take a pic and capture your deep, deep Judy discovery."
jokes.setup: Abbi's face registers shock at Judge Judy's net worth
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately switches to documenting the moment rather than sharing it, and names it a 'Judy discovery' as though it's an anthropological event
jokes.explanation: This sets up the matrix/phone addiction theme that drives the episode. 'Deep, deep Judy discovery' is a funny phrase. Ilana's instinct to photograph rather than engage is both in-character and thematically relevant.

jokes.text: "I'm gonna 'gram it. No filter." — followed by the 'Whoa...' beat implying the phone sucks them into a vortex
jokes.setup: Ilana photographs Abbi's face and announces she's posting it to Instagram
jokes.punchline: The single word 'Whoa...' at 03:03 followed by a long silence until 03:43 implies they completely lost themselves in the phone vortex — the comedy is entirely in the implication of the 40-second gap
jokes.explanation: This is structurally inventive — the episode uses dead air and a time jump to dramatize phone addiction. The 40-second gap in the transcript IS the joke. 'Whoa...' is the last word before they disappear into the matrix, and the subsequent 'Jesus, I forgot you were in the room!' reveals they lost each other while sitting next to each other. Earns a high craft score for using the episode format itself as a comedic tool.

jokes.text: "Jesus, I forgot you were in the (Bleep) room! Holy (Bleep), dude, how did we forget that we were here together?"
jokes.setup: The 40-second phone vortex gap
jokes.punchline: They have become so absorbed in their phones that they forgot the other person was physically present in the same room — a mutual forgetting that shocks both of them
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's first major laugh — the payoff of the time-jump setup. Two best friends who are physically next to each other forgetting each other exist is a sharp satirical point about phone addiction delivered through pure character comedy. The mutual nature of the forgetting (not one of them, both) is what elevates it.

jokes.text: "We were so tapped into the worldwide bloodstream, we fell into the lint roll matrix."
jokes.setup: They're trying to understand how they both forgot each other while sitting together
jokes.punchline: Ilana's explanation invokes pseudo-mystical techno-language — 'worldwide bloodstream' and 'lint roll matrix' — to describe getting lost on their phones
jokes.explanation: Episode title drop delivered perfectly in character. 'Worldwide bloodstream' is an unexpectedly evocative phrase for phone addiction, and 'lint roll matrix' is wonderfully specific and absurd — tying the mundane (lint rolling) to a grandiose Matrix metaphor. Deeply Ilana: her explanations always reach for cosmic significance. High craft score justified by originality and character specificity.

jokes.text: "Gmail, Grindr, Facebook, Facetime, Insta, Grindr, Tumblr, Twitter, Dlisted."
jokes.setup: Ilana is listing all the apps she's plugged into daily
jokes.punchline: Grindr appears TWICE in the list — a gay hookup app listed among the standard social media, and its double appearance suggests she uses it so much she mentioned it twice without noticing
jokes.explanation: The double Grindr is the hidden gem of this list — it's both a character joke (Ilana's fluid sexuality and enthusiastic use of a gay men's hookup app) and a structural joke (repeating it implies she uses it twice as much as everything else, or that she listed it so naturally she forgot she'd said it). The inclusion of Dlisted (a gossip blog) as a peer to Gmail is also funny in its specificity. Earns high craft: economy is excellent, character integration is perfect.

jokes.text: "...rip the plug out to go to sleep, rub one out, rinse and repeat."
jokes.setup: Ilana is describing the daily digital routine
jokes.punchline: She includes 'rub one out' as a standard step in the bedtime routine, sandwiched between wholesome activities and delivered without any shame
jokes.explanation: Classic Ilana overshare — the shock comes from the casual delivery and the alliterative 'rinse and repeat' that follows. The alliteration makes it feel almost wholesome until you process what she said. Character integration is excellent; the joke itself isn't novel.

jokes.text: Ilana answering her phone mid-speech about how they're addicted to their phones
jokes.setup: Ilana is mid-monologue about how they are slaves to technology and auto-respond to it
jokes.punchline: Her phone rings and she immediately, automatically answers it — demonstrating the exact auto-response she was describing, without any apparent self-awareness
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's sharpest structural gag. The setup is literally Ilana mid-sentence talking about auto-responses, and the punchline is her auto-responding. It's practically a textbook irony example but executed perfectly — the timing is everything and it's earned because we've been building to this theme. High craft justified: structure is near-perfect, economy maxes out (the joke requires zero words to land), and it's pure character truth.

jokes.text: "Dude, I'm not my brother's keeper." in response to whether Lincoln is wearing a tie
jokes.setup: Eliot calls to ask if Lincoln (Ilana's brother) is wearing a tie to the dog wedding
jokes.punchline: Ilana invokes the biblical 'am I my brother's keeper?' to refuse to know her brother's outfit — the grandiose biblical citation for the trivial subject of tie selection
jokes.explanation: Functional joke — the biblical reference applied to fashion is a reliable register-clash move. Nothing exceptional but well-delivered.

jokes.text: "They're on Abbi's toes." — the wedding rings are being worn on Abbi's toes
jokes.setup: Eliot asks if Ilana remembered the wedding rings
jokes.punchline: She has the rings — but they're being worn on Abbi's toes, which is presented as completely normal storage
jokes.explanation: Absurdist gem. The joke works because it's presented with zero explanation or justification — Ilana says 'They're on Abbi's toes' as though this is the obvious place to put wedding rings. This also sets up the later visual payoff when we see the rings. Very economical and perfectly in character for the show's chaotic logic.

jokes.text: "Not to 'patronize' you, but of course I have the rings" — Ilana air-quotes 'patronize' back at Eliot
jokes.setup: Eliot said 'I'm not patronizing you, but did you remember the rings?'
jokes.punchline: Ilana weaponizes Eliot's own word choice back at him while immediately revealing the rings are on someone's toes
jokes.explanation: Good small beat — the irony of her indignation immediately followed by revealing the rings are on Abbi's toes plays well. The air-quote-back is a familiar move but timed correctly here.

jokes.text: The rollerblades are Jaime's, acquired after Hurricane Wanda for 'future emergencies'
jokes.setup: Abbi suggests they rollerblade to the wedding to take in more nature
jokes.punchline: The blades belong to their roommate and were purchased post-hurricane as emergency gear — rollerblades as disaster preparedness equipment is the absurd premise
jokes.explanation: Solid world-building joke that also characterizes Jaime. 'Hurricane Wanda' as the name is a nice detail. The practical problem of the blades being too big for Abbi is set up here, which pays off in injury.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana negotiating how to fill Ilana's oversized rollerblades — 'sponges, cotton balls, newspaper around my negative space in my foot'
jokes.setup: The blades are too big for Abbi's feet
jokes.punchline: Their solutions escalate in absurdity: newspaper, sponges, cotton balls — described as filling 'my negative space in my foot or something'
jokes.explanation: Functional scene-setting comedy. 'Negative space in my foot' is a funny phrase. Nothing exceptional.

jokes.text: The blades actually fit Ilana — 'They fit me. They're a little snug, actually.' — followed by Abbi's deflated 'That's cool... That's cool... Yeah. That's cool.'
jokes.setup: They assumed the blades would be too big for Ilana given they've been debating how to fit Abbi into them
jokes.punchline: The blades fit Ilana perfectly, even snugly — implying Abbi has unusually small/thin feet. Abbi's triple 'That's cool' is the cringe payoff — she's clearly bothered but performing acceptance
jokes.explanation: The triple 'That's cool' is perfectly performed cringe — three repetitions with diminishing conviction, each more clearly lying than the last. Abbi's specific brand of forced positivity is well-served here. Good character work.

jokes.text: Neither of them knows where the dog wedding is — they don't have their phones
jokes.setup: They've left their phones behind in a bid to go off the grid
jokes.punchline: They don't know where they're going
jokes.explanation: Expected consequence played straight — the joke is in the obviousness of what happens when you leave your phone and don't write anything down. Functional plot mechanism.

jokes.text: "If our foremothers can find countries without phones, we can find a dang dog wedding."
jokes.setup: Abbi wants to go back for their phones since they don't know where the wedding is
jokes.punchline: Ilana invokes 'foremothers' finding COUNTRIES without phones as equivalent to finding a dog wedding in Central Park — scale collapse is the joke, plus 'dang' as a mild oath
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ilana line — the feminist framing ('foremothers'), the grandiose scale ('finding countries'), and the deflation ('dang dog wedding') all land perfectly. The feminist reframe of navigation history applied to finding a party is pure Ilana voice. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: "I miss the blue light. Ooh, I wanna marry the blue light."
jokes.setup: Ilana insists they can go hours without phones
jokes.punchline: Abbi anthropomorphizes her phone withdrawal — she doesn't just miss her phone, she wants to marry the screen glow
jokes.explanation: Good escalation on phone dependency — from 'miss' to 'marry' in one line. The specificity of 'the blue light' rather than the phone itself is the right detail, making it feel like actual addiction language.

jokes.text: "If I had my phone right now, I would Tinder the (Bleep) out of like all those beefcakes."
jokes.setup: They spot attractive shirtless men in the park and appreciate them
jokes.punchline: Ilana's instinct isn't to approach them but to mediate the attraction through an app — even when the actual men are standing right in front of her
jokes.explanation: Sharp point about app-mediated desire made through character comedy. The irony is that Tinder is unnecessary in this exact situation. Sets up the IRL Tinder bit well.

jokes.text: "You don't need Tinder for these dudes. You've got Tinder right here, IRL. Wanna 'fook'?!"
jokes.setup: Ilana just said she'd Tinder the beefcakes if she had her phone
jokes.punchline: She immediately demonstrates 'IRL Tinder' by shouting 'Wanna fook?!' at strangers in the park — the clarification that it's small ('wanna fook?') makes it worse somehow
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The conceptual leap from 'Tinder but IRL' to just shouting at people is funny on its own, but the execution — 'Wanna fook?!' then the clarification 'Oh it's not big, it's like wanna fook?' — is brilliant. The lowercase soft delivery instruction after the all-caps shout is a perfect double-beat. Deeply Ilana, highly quotable, genuinely original. Earns 8+ for character integration and structural originality.

jokes.text: Abbi being peer-pressured into yelling 'Wanna foooook?' in the park
jokes.setup: Ilana has already yelled at strangers; Abbi refuses and calls her insane; Ilana invokes 'foremothers' logic
jokes.punchline: Abbi eventually yells it — drawn out, agonized — and it (apparently) works
jokes.explanation: The payoff works because of Abbi's specific reluctance and the drawn-out, tortured 'fooooook' she produces — it sounds nothing like Ilana's confident version and yet somehow it works. The contrast between Ilana's casual confidence and Abbi's mortification is the Broad City dynamic in miniature.

jokes.text: Abbi falls while rollerblading immediately after the 'fook' success — 'My first impression is always better on Tinder. I suck without the Internet, dude.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just successfully yelled 'wanna fook' at a man and it worked
jokes.punchline: She immediately wipes out physically, then philosophizes: 'My first impression is always better on Tinder. I suck without the Internet, dude.'
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels simultaneously — the physical comedy of the wipeout puncturing the triumph, and the line itself, which is a genuinely sharp observation about how people curate their digital personas. 'I suck without the Internet' is quotable and true in a way that extends beyond Abbi. Well-structured and thematically resonant.

jokes.text: The 'bail! bail!' sequence — Ilana and Abbi discover they can't stop their rollerblades and bail off-road into a ditch/hole
jokes.setup: They're rollerblading through the park with oversized blades
jokes.punchline: 'We're gonna have to bail' — 'What do you mean, bail?' — 'Bail!' — 'What do you mean, bail?' — 'Oh (Bleep), I can't stop!'
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'bail/what do you mean bail' creates a nice escalating panic rhythm. The physical gag of not being able to stop rollerblades in oversized equipment is set up well by the earlier blade-fitting scene. Solid physical comedy.

jokes.text: 'What if I jump down in there, and then we scream for help together?' / 'No, you need to go get help.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is stuck in a hole. They have no phones.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's instinct is to jump IN the hole too and then they can both scream — which is completely the wrong instinct and also reveals she'd rather be with Abbi than be useful.
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific. Ilana's instinct is always togetherness over practicality — her best idea in a crisis is 'we'll be stuck together.' The fact that she floats this as a genuine plan and Abbi has to patiently say no is a perfect microcosm of their dynamic.

jokes.text: Abbi stuck in a hole — Ilana's emergency kit is 'two joints, nuts and figs, and a lanyard'
jokes.setup: Abbi is injured and stuck in a hole without a phone
jokes.punchline: Ilana produces her emergency supplies: two joints for pain management, nuts and figs for sustenance, and a lanyard 'for entertainment'
jokes.explanation: The escalating absurdity of the emergency kit is excellent: the joints for pain management is a funny but internally logical Ilana solution, nuts and figs for sustenance is earnest survivalist, and the lanyard 'for entertainment' is the killer escalation. It's three jokes in a tight list. Deeply character-specific — only Ilana packs this. Earns high craft for character integration and economy.

jokes.text: "You mean gimp?" / "You wanna argue about what it's called right now? You, in the hole?"
jokes.setup: Ilana says she has 'lanyard for entertainment'
jokes.punchline: Abbi corrects her that it's called 'gimp'; Ilana's shutdown — 'You, in the hole?' — is the punchline
jokes.explanation: 'You, in the hole?' is a genuinely quotable put-down — three words that perfectly shut down pedantry from someone in a worse position. Great economy. 'Gimp' also carries comedic weight as a word.

jokes.text: "Okay, I'm gonna BRB."
jokes.setup: Ilana is leaving Abbi injured in a hole to go find help
jokes.punchline: She uses 'BRB' — a text-message abbreviation — in spoken, face-to-face conversation as a farewell, having just talked about being addicted to the internet
jokes.explanation: Tight callback to the 'we have auto-responses programmed' speech — Ilana speaks in internet abbreviations even when off the grid, demonstrating she can't actually escape the matrix. Economy is near-perfect. 'BRB' said in a genuine emergency is also just inherently funny.

jokes.text: "I don't want these figs. I don't like the little beads in 'em."
jokes.setup: Ilana has left figs for Abbi's sustenance while she's trapped in a hole
jokes.punchline: Abbi rejects the survival food on the grounds of texture preference — 'the little beads' — while stuck in a hole with a sprained ankle
jokes.explanation: Perfect mundane complaint in an extreme situation. The specificity of 'the little beads in 'em' is what makes it — it's a real complaint about figs. Then the reversal at 10:07 ('Wait, Ilana — I do want the figs') is a lovely small beat that shows Abbi reconsidering in her isolation.

jokes.text: "Wait, Ilana. [pause] Yeah? [pause] I do want the figs."
jokes.setup: Abbi just rejected the figs on texture grounds
jokes.punchline: She calls Ilana back — building momentary false suspense — to admit she does want them after all
jokes.explanation: The comedic pause structure is excellent: the callback of her name ('Wait, Ilana') builds expectation that something important will be said, and the deflation to 'I do want the figs' is a perfect small joke. 'Good choice' as Ilana's response seals it. Maximum comedy with minimum words.

jokes.text: Someone at the wedding nearly eats the dog quiche — 'Those are the dog quiche!'
jokes.setup: Wedding preparation scene; someone reaches for food
jokes.punchline: The existence of separate dog quiche (and the possibility of mix-up with human quiche) establishes the comedic logic of the dog wedding world
jokes.explanation: Good world-establishing joke. The quiche mix-up is a reliable comedic premise (human accidentally eating pet food, or vice versa) but the specificity of dog quiche is funnier than dog kibble.

jokes.text: "I just checked in with the bride. Her heart is racing, but that's not unusual. Chihuahuas' hearts always race. They were bred as like a joke."
jokes.setup: The wedding planner is checking on the nervous bride
jokes.punchline: The bride's anxiety is explained as species-specific, then punctured by 'They were bred as like a joke' — the most casual dismissal of an entire breed's existence
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan line. 'They were bred as like a joke' is highly quotable and the delivery (presumably straight-faced by the wedding professional) makes it funnier. The 'like' in the middle is perfect — it's not a confident assertion, it's an off-hand aside.

jokes.text: "Cold paws is a very serious disease in the chihuahua community. Poor toe circulation. It's the number-one killer of chihuahuas."
jokes.setup: The wedding planner mentions the bride might have cold paws (a pun on cold feet)
jokes.punchline: Lincoln delivers fabricated veterinary statistics about 'cold paws' as a real disease — 'number-one killer of chihuahuas' — with full authority
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist straight-faced medical authority. The joke works because Lincoln is a veterinarian so the false expertise feels plausible for a half-second before you realize he's making up dog statistics.

jokes.text: 'Cold paws is a very serious disease in the chihuahua community. Poor toe circulation. It's the number-one killer of chihuahuas.'
jokes.setup: Eliot mentions the bride might have 'cold paws' (the dog equivalent of cold feet).
jokes.punchline: Lincoln doubles down with completely fabricated medical authority — 'Cold paws is a very serious disease... it's the number-one killer of chihuahuas' — presented as veterinary fact.
jokes.explanation: Builds beautifully on the joke above. Lincoln is a vet, so the fake medical authority has extra ironic weight — he's made up a disease. 'The number-one killer of chihuahuas' is the escalation that makes it. The echo of 'bred as like a joke' → 'cold paws kills them' suggests Lincoln has a whole theory about chihuahuas as cosmic mistake.

jokes.text: "Cold paws, cold feet, you get why I..." trailing off
jokes.setup: Lincoln just declared cold paws is the number-one killer of chihuahuas
jokes.punchline: The wedding planner explains she made a pun — 'cold paws, cold feet' — but the delivery is embarrassed and trails off, as if she regrets the joke
jokes.explanation: The meta-joke is that she's explaining a pun that didn't need explaining, and the trail-off shows she knows it. Functional but slight.

jokes.text: Eliot sweating through his suit, worried Brandon will see his 'enormous sweat glands'
jokes.setup: Eliot is panicking about the wedding logistics
jokes.punchline: His concern pivots from professional ('this is a disaster') to personal vanity ('I need you to block me from Brandon so he doesn't see my enormous sweat glands')
jokes.explanation: 'Enormous sweat glands' is a funny phrase — medical language applied to something embarrassingly human. The pivot from professional concern to personal vanity is good character work.

jokes.text: "I've never been to a dog wedding before, but I'd get married here in a second. Hey, don't tell Ilana I said that."
jokes.setup: Lincoln is reassuring Eliot about the venue
jokes.punchline: He accidentally confesses he'd get married (implying to Ilana), then immediately panics and asks for secrecy — a slip that reveals romantic feelings he's not ready to act on
jokes.explanation: Good character moment — Lincoln's genuine feelings for Ilana slipping out in the context of a dog wedding. 'I'm just running my mouth' as a cover is classically awkward. The joke works because the relationship dynamic is real.

jokes.text: Abbi's pain-induced fantasy sequence — she imagines herself 'down the shore with my family', then escalates to increasingly glamorous scenarios with celebrities
jokes.setup: Abbi is using visualization to manage her pain
jokes.punchline: She escalates from realistic ('down the shore with my family') through 'dinner with Elijah Wood' (specific celebrity) to 'at an extravagant flea market with Mark Ruffalo' shopping for wooden milk crates, to 'Turks and Caicos with Taye Diggs' — each iteration more specific and absurd
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best extended sequences. The comedy escalates perfectly: each fantasy gets more specific and more revealing. The Elijah Wood one is unexpected. The Mark Ruffalo flea market scenario has added depth — 'wooden milk crates to line our office/maybe nursery one day' — then immediately: 'But we don't say that to everybody' — this is a whole character study hidden in a pain fantasy. The Stella-referencing Taye Diggs finale ('There's no pain when you're getting your groove back') is the perfect button. High craft: originality, structure, and character integration all earn high marks. The visual gags (Abbi is in a hole during all of this) add to impact.

jokes.text: Ilana gets completely lost in the park looking for the dog wedding and we hear her calling 'Eliot!' into the void
jokes.setup: Ilana confidently set off on rollerblades to find the wedding and get help
jokes.punchline: The long timestamp gap from 13:08 to 13:54 with just her calling 'Eliot!' repeatedly implies she's been wandering the park completely lost — the confident 'foremother' who found countries cannot find a dog wedding
jokes.explanation: Good structural callback — the foremothers speech is paid off by Ilana completely failing to navigate without GPS. The visual of her rollerblading around Central Park yelling into the empty air is strong physical comedy inferred from the transcript.

jokes.text: Ilana stumbles upon something strange in the park — the timestamp gaps and 'Whoa.' / 'What is this?' / 'Oh, Jesus, turn it off.' / 'What the (Bleep)?' reaction sequence
jokes.setup: Ilana is wandering lost in the park
jokes.punchline: She encounters something progressively disturbing — her reactions escalate from wonder to horror to confusion, entirely conveyed through one-liners with no explanation
jokes.explanation: This is primarily a visual gag sequence that doesn't translate fully to transcript form, but the escalating single-line reactions ('Whoa' → 'What is this?' → 'Oh, Jesus, turn it off' → 'What the (Bleep)?') create good comedic rhythm. The show likely delivers the visual payoff on screen. High rewatch bonus as the transcript version is incomplete.

jokes.text: "Look, I don't want to freak out right now, but that bitch, she won't keep her dress on. It's a Vera Wag."
jokes.setup: The dog bride keeps removing her wedding dress, causing panic
jokes.punchline: 'Vera Wag' as the dog-wedding equivalent of Vera Wang — the brand pun lands while also conveying the genuine distress of someone whose Vera Wang-caliber dress is being refused
jokes.explanation: 'Vera Wag' is a tight pun that works on delivery. The combination of the genuine frustration about the dress and the absurd brand name is the right mix. Highly quotable in context.

jokes.text: "Ordinarily, I would not say this, but they're dogs. / Well, you just lost one star on dog Yelp."
jokes.setup: The officiant suggests proceeding without the rings
jokes.punchline: Lincoln threatens to leave a bad Yelp review on 'dog Yelp' — an invented platform for dog event vendors — as a genuine threat
jokes.explanation: 'Dog Yelp' is a great phrase — it's instantly clear, implies a whole absurd ecosystem, and Lincoln's use of it as a genuine threat is perfect. The line is extremely economical and highly quotable. One of the tighter jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Negotiating with the dog bride about whether she's actually into the groom — 'I can't afford more alimony'
jokes.setup: The dog bride Poppi keeps taking off her dress — possibly getting cold feet
jokes.punchline: Eliot has a serious conversation with the dog about whether she really wants to marry Atticus, citing prior alimony payments as a financial concern
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of dog alimony is committed to fully — the show treats the dog marriage with genuine emotional stakes, which is the right choice for this kind of comedy. 'I can't afford more alimony' implies this dog has been previously married and divorced.

jokes.text: "Oh, dog wedding gazebo! Yes! Maps rule! Foremother's a bitch! Sacagawea!"
jokes.setup: Ilana has been lost in the park; she finally finds the wedding location using... a park map
jokes.punchline: Her joy at finding the gazebo via a physical map explodes into a four-beat celebration: (1) she found it, (2) maps rule (she needed a paper map, not GPS), (3) 'foremother's a bitch' — she's retracting her earlier praise of foremothers for navigating without technology, (4) 'Sacagawea!' as the specific foremother being insulted
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke. A perfect multi-layer callback: Ilana praised foremothers for navigating without technology, got completely lost without technology, and upon finding a MAP (not a phone), both validates and revises her position. The progression of four exclamations is beautifully structured — each one lands separately and each one escalates. 'Foremother's a bitch!' is the comedic peak; 'Sacagawea!' as the specific target is the button. This is the kind of joke that earns 8+ — it requires the entire episode's setup to land, it's deeply character-specific, highly quotable, and structurally earned. The irony of using a physical map as the solution (off-grid, old-school navigation) while still cursing out the foremothers is the final layer.

jokes.text: Tourists find Ilana rollerblading and one of them photographs her on a phone — she first tries to give them her number ('Are you gonna call?') then realizes they're just photographing her
jokes.setup: Ilana rollerblades up to strangers to ask for help
jokes.punchline: She misreads someone holding up a phone as them wanting her number — 'Are you gonna call?' — then realizes they're just photographing her as a curiosity
jokes.explanation: Nice character beat — Ilana's default assumption upon seeing a phone pointed at her is that someone wants to connect romantically. The humiliation of realizing she's just a tourist curiosity plays well. Connects to the phone-matrix theme.

jokes.text: "Sir... I'm afraid we're never gonna get out of this place."
jokes.setup: Abbi is stuck in a hole, relying on a non-English-speaking stranger's phone
jokes.punchline: After the communication breakdown, Abbi delivers a theatrical existential statement — 'I'm afraid we're never gonna get out of this place' — to someone who can't understand her anyway
jokes.explanation: Small existential beat. The humor is in the scale — 'we're never gonna get out of this place' about being in a hole in Central Park for less than an hour. Delivered to a non-English speaker amplifies the pointlessness.

jokes.text: 'Sir... I'm afraid we're never gonna get out of this place.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is apparently trying to communicate with someone (perhaps the same person who doesn't speak English, or another park stranger).
jokes.punchline: 'I'm afraid we're never gonna get out of this place' — said with the gravity of someone trapped on a desert island, not stuck in Central Park.
jokes.explanation: The melodrama of 'I'm afraid we're never gonna get out of this place' applied to a NYC park is a solid line. The 'we' is also interesting — she may be addressing someone, or herself. Good deadpan understatement.

jokes.text: Ilana arrives at the wedding mid-ceremony shouting 'Wait! Hold up! Stop the wedding!' — and someone mutters 'She's always gotta make it about her.'
jokes.setup: The officiant is mid-ceremony; the wedding is finally happening without the rings
jokes.punchline: Ilana bursts in dramatically; a guest mutters 'She's always gotta make it about her' — implying this is a known pattern of Ilana's behavior, even at a dog wedding
jokes.explanation: The guest's muttered aside is the best part — it implies this person knows Ilana well enough to predict her behavior, and the fact that it's happening at a dog wedding makes the mundane social observation absurd. Good character comedy from a throwaway line.

jokes.text: The dog identifies Abbi's scent on Ilana's repurposed shirt — 'This was once Abbi's shirt that I re-purposed to make cooler. Come on, boy, that's her scent.' / 'She's a girl.' / 'Oh. We'll treat her differently, then.'
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives with Abbi's shirt to lead a dog back to Abbi's location
jokes.punchline: The dog shirt was repurposed without Abbi's input; Lincoln corrects the dog's gender; Ilana's 'We'll treat her differently, then' implies some behavioral recalibration for a female dog — the specificity of that commitment is the joke
jokes.explanation: Good layered beat. The shirt-repurposing is a small Ilana character note. The gender correction and 'We'll treat her differently, then' suggests Ilana has gendered protocols for canine search-and-rescue.

jokes.text: Ilana doing a Simon Cowell impression at the dog wedding, critiquing a dog's 'performance'
jokes.setup: At the wedding, apparently a dog has 'performed' something
jokes.punchline: Ilana delivers TV talent-show judge feedback — 'Brazil nut, I gotta say your pitch is fantastic. You have some star quality. I can't speak for the other judges but we'll see what they say.' — to a dog
jokes.explanation: Ilana doing a talent show impression mid-rescue at a dog wedding is a good absurdist detour. 'Brazil nut' as a dog nickname is a nice choice. The impression itself is competent but the situation is what makes it funny.

jokes.text: Ilana arguing with another 'judge' about the dog's performance — 'She put her heart out there. She put her soul out there.' — then immediately snapping out of it
jokes.setup: Ilana is doing a talent show judge bit
jokes.punchline: She escalates into genuine emotional investment in the dog's talent show performance, getting into a heated argument, then snaps back to reality when she hears Abbi's name
jokes.explanation: The escalation from casual impression to heated advocacy is the joke. Ilana gets sucked into a bit the same way she got sucked into the phone matrix. The snap back to 'Abbi!' when she hears her friend's name is the right button.

jokes.text: 'No, I completely disagree. Are you kidding me? She put her heart out there. She put her soul out there. You are completely overlooking the level of commitment that performance took!'
jokes.setup: Another judge (or the reality show format) has apparently given a less favorable assessment of the dog.
jokes.punchline: Ilana erupts in passionate defense, applying Simon vs. Paula mentor-style TV drama to a dog's performance at its own wedding.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from polite judge feedback to full talent show emotional defense is perfect Ilana. 'She put her heart out there. She put her soul out there.' applied to a dog at a wedding is the payoff. The character integration is excellent — this is specifically Ilana's passion and her tendency to turn everything into a performance.

jokes.text: "Hold up, hold up, hold up, hold up. Abbi's wearing my backpack as a diaper."
jokes.setup: Ilana was gone for what felt like much longer than 25 minutes; she returns to find Abbi
jokes.punchline: Abbi has fashioned Ilana's backpack into a diaper — the visual of this is the joke, and Ilana's 'hold up x4' implies genuine confusion before naming what she's seeing
jokes.explanation: Strong visual gag that we can infer perfectly from transcript. Four 'hold ups' before the reveal creates perfect comedic pacing. 'Wearing my backpack as a diaper' is a fantastically specific and unexpected development — how did Abbi get there? The implied answer (she was stuck in a hole for half an hour with nothing to do) is funnier than any explanation.

jokes.text: "Dude, I was gone for like 25 minutes. Half-hour tops. / It felt like a lot longer."
jokes.setup: Abbi is wearing a backpack as a diaper after being left alone
jokes.punchline: Ilana's defensive '25 minutes, half-hour tops' meets Abbi's weary 'It felt like a lot longer' — the disconnect between Ilana's breezy timeline and the diaper situation is the joke
jokes.explanation: The 'it felt like a lot longer' with a backpack-diaper as visual evidence is a good deadpan exchange. The understatement from both characters lands.

jokes.text: "I'm practically a doctor." / "But you're not." / "I am a doctor, and I have a lot of experience with sprains and breaks." / "No offense, but it's more like you drain fluid from their butt sacks."
jokes.setup: Lincoln, a veterinarian, is assessing Abbi's injury
jokes.punchline: Abbi's 'I'm practically a doctor' is shut down; Lincoln claims actual doctor status; Abbi's 'you drain fluid from their butt sacks' reduces his medical expertise to its most undignified element
jokes.explanation: 'You drain fluid from their butt sacks' is the episode's sharpest line outside the Sacagawea moment. It's specific, true (vet medicine does include anal gland expression), degrading in the most precise way, and lands as a perfect put-down. The sequence is well-structured: Abbi's overclaim → Lincoln's legitimate counter → Abbi's killer reframe. High craft for originality and character integration — only Abbi would know this specific fact and deploy it.

jokes.text: "Look, the truth about cats and dogs is..." — Lincoln starts a movie-title setup that's immediately cut off
jokes.setup: Lincoln is attempting to defend veterinary medicine
jokes.punchline: He begins what sounds like a reference to the movie 'The Truth About Cats and Dogs' but is cut off before completing the thought — the joke is the setup that never delivers
jokes.explanation: Slight meta gag that works mainly because it's interrupted. The interruption before the punchline is funnier than whatever the punchline would have been.

jokes.text: The wedding ring conversation — 'You'll get them when the swelling goes down.' / '48 hours.' / 'In about one to two weeks.'
jokes.setup: The dog wedding rings are on Abbi's toes and her foot is injured and swollen — they're trying to figure out when she can have them back
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's optimistic '48 hours' is immediately revised to 'one to two weeks' — a significant expansion of timeline delivered without any acknowledgment of how different those estimates are
jokes.explanation: Good callback payoff to the rings-on-toes setup. The escalation from 48 hours to two weeks without any transition is the joke. Also pays off the visual of the rings on swollen feet, which we've been waiting to see since the toes reveal.

jokes.text: "By the powers vested in me by the state of New York, I now pronounce you, dog husband, and dog wife." / "Ok, kiss."
jokes.setup: The dog wedding ceremony finally completes after all the chaos
jokes.punchline: The legal language — 'powers vested in me by the state of New York' — applied to dogs, followed by the completely flat 'Ok, kiss.' to prompt a dog kiss
jokes.explanation: 'Ok, kiss.' is the right deflation after the legal formality. The show has committed to treating the dog wedding with full ceremonial seriousness throughout, and this is the payoff of that commitment — the legal language stands, only the subject is ridiculous.

jokes.text: "So I guess we're related now. / Can in-laws hook up? / Yeah, it's like soft incest. / Whoa, that's so royal."
jokes.setup: The dogs are now married, making Eliot and Lincoln dog in-laws
jokes.punchline: They immediately discuss whether in-laws can hook up — framed as 'soft incest' — and Eliot finds this exciting because it's 'royal'
jokes.explanation: Callback to the 'cocaine and incest, baby' opener — the episode bookends with incest jokes. 'Soft incest' as a term is original and funny, and 'that's so royal' as Eliot's endorsement is the perfect button. The chain of logic is impeccable: dogs married → we're in-laws → can we hook up → soft incest → royal. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: "I think I've seen all I needed to see today. Take her outta here, boys."
jokes.setup: Someone just described hooking up with their dog's in-law as 'soft incest' and 'royal'
jokes.punchline: A bystander delivers a mob-boss-style 'I've seen all I needed to see' — the language of someone who has witnessed either a crime or a revelation — and asks to leave
jokes.explanation: Good button for the scene. The theatrical exit language over a soft-incest conversation is a funny tonal mismatch. 'Take her outta here, boys' implies he arrived with an entourage, which is its own minor joke.

jokes.text: Final shot: Abbi, in an ambulance, eating a fig and saying 'Fig!'
jokes.setup: Abbi rejected figs, then accepted figs, then was stuck in a hole all episode with only figs and a gimp/lanyard
jokes.punchline: The final word of the episode is 'Fig!' — delivered with the pleasure of someone who has earned this fig through suffering. A perfect, wordless summation of the episode.
jokes.explanation: Pitch-perfect episode button. The single word 'Fig!' earns its high score through cumulative setup: Abbi rejected figs (for valid texture reasons), then reconsidered, was left with them as her only comfort through a half-hour ordeal in a hole, and now in the ambulance — rescued, rings on swollen toes, wearing a backpack-diaper — she eats one and it's delicious. The economy score is a 10 because a single exclaimed food word closes an entire episode arc. This is great sitcom writing — the laugh comes from the story, not a punchline.
